name: Courthouse Wash
short_description: Scenic BLM wash near Moab offering accessible agate and jasper collecting in colorful badlands terrain. Personal use rockhounding with no permit required.
description:
Courthouse Wash is a popular BLM rockhounding destination in eastern Utah near Moab, offering easy access to colorful agate, jasper, and chalcedony specimens. The wash cuts through distinctive red and tan badlands formations typical of the Colorado Plateau, creating excellent opportunities for surface collecting. This area is ideal for beginners and families seeking attractive specimens without technical climbing or difficult terrain.

The geology here reflects the Morrison Formation and overlying Jurassic sediments, which contain abundant chert and chalcedony nodules deposited in ancient lake and river environments. The exposed walls and wash floor of Courthouse Wash provide excellent access to these formations, and seasonal water flow helps expose fresh material. Collectors consistently find banded agates, red and orange jasper, and translucent chalcedony pieces ranging from thumbnail to fist size.

As BLM public land, Courthouse Wash requires no permits for personal use rockhounding within the standard 25 lb daily limit. The wash is accessible via a well-marked route from US 191, making it one of the most convenient collecting sites near Moab. The setting offers scenic views of arches and canyon country while remaining relatively sheltered by the wash environment.

Expect moderate walking on sandy and rocky wash floor with some climbing over minor obstacles. The terrain is not technical but does require sturdy footwear and sun protection. Water is not available on site, so bring adequate supplies. Spring and fall offer the most comfortable collecting conditions, though the site remains accessible year-round due to the region's generally dry climate.
history: Courthouse Wash sits within the Colorado Plateau's distinctive badlands, where Jurassic-age Morrison Formation sediments and overlying Cretaceous rocks were deposited in shallow marine and fluvial environments 140 to 100 million years ago. Silica-rich groundwater percolating through these sediments formed the agate and jasper nodules found here today, concentrating in layers that are now exposed by the modern wash's erosion through the colorful badlands.
tips: Search the wash floor and exposed bank faces for chalcedony nodules half-buried in sand and gravel, particularly after water flow has recently scoured the channel. Look for agate material in the red and tan exposed strata on wash walls, especially where softer sediments have weathered away. Use a small pry bar to test loose nodules in the walls, but never hammer directly at the exposed formations. Focus effort in side drainages and low areas where material naturally concentulates due to water transport.
terrain_notes: Courthouse Wash follows a sandy to rocky streambed through colorful badlands terrain at approximately 3,900 feet elevation. The wash walls display dramatic red, tan, and brown sedimentary layers typical of the Colorado Plateau. Walking is generally easy on the wash floor with minor obstacles and occasional small drops. Vegetation is sparse to moderate, consisting of juniper, sage, and hardy shrubs. The collecting area extends roughly 0.5 to 1.5 miles up the wash from the main access point, with increasingly rough terrain further upstream. Footing is uneven but not treacherous for hikers accustomed to desert walking.
best_season: October - April. Desert badlands terrain offers extreme heat exposure in summer months (May-September), with daytime temperatures regularly exceeding 95 degrees Fahrenheit. Fall and spring provide comfortable collecting conditions with mild temperatures and lower sun intensity. Winter visits are possible but early mornings can be cold; the site rarely accumulates snow at this elevation.
written_directions: From Moab, take US 191 north for approximately 17 miles. Turn right onto Utah State Road 313 heading toward Canyonlands National Park. Continue for approximately 4 miles and look for the unsigned Courthouse Wash pullout on the right side of the road. The pullout provides access to a short walk to the wash proper. Alternatively, from central Moab, drive north on US 191 and watch for the Courthouse Wash trailhead sign approximately 16 miles north of town. A high-clearance passenger vehicle can reach the pullout, but 4x4 vehicles have better access to additional side drainages if conditions are muddy.
road_conditions: Utah State Road 313 is paved to the Courthouse Wash area. The access pullout and short approach roads may be unimproved dirt with light washboard. Passenger vehicles with moderate ground clearance can access the main parking area in most conditions.
parking_notes: Unsigned dirt pullout on the right side of State Road 313 with capacity for approximately 3-5 vehicles. No facilities present. Parking area is minimally developed and can be muddy after rain.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment use. Respect all public land regulations. Do not hammer or damage formation walls; collect loose material and nodules only. Pack out all trash.
rules:
Personal use only - no commercial collection
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No motorized excavation or power tools
Collect loose material and nodules only
No hammering at formation walls
Pack out all trash
Respect other rockhounders and hikers
nearest_services: Moab, approximately 17 miles south. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and water. No services at the site itself.
accessibility_notes: Relatively easy terrain accessible to most hikers and families with basic fitness. Flat to gently sloping wash floor with occasional small rocks and minor obstacles. Uneven sandy and rocky walking surface requires sturdy footwear. No wheelchair access to the wash itself. The short approach walk from parking is manageable for people with typical hiking ability. Exposed terrain offers minimal shade; sun protection is essential.

faq.question: What types of rocks and gems can you find rockhounding at Courthouse Wash near Moab?
faq.answer: Courthouse Wash is known for producing agate, jasper, chalcedony, and occasional petrified wood specimens. Most pieces are found as loose nodules on the wash floor or weathering from the exposed badlands walls. Collectors typically find specimens ranging from thumbnail to fist size, with the best pieces showing banded patterns in red, orange, tan, and brown colors.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at Courthouse Wash in Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding on BLM land at Courthouse Wash. You can collect freely within the standard 25 lb daily limit per FLPMA regulations. This makes it one of the most accessible and affordable rockhounding sites near Moab with no fees or paperwork.

faq.question: What is the best season to rockhound for agate at Courthouse Wash?
faq.answer: October through April provides the most comfortable collecting conditions with mild temperatures and lower sun exposure. Summer months (May-September) can be extremely hot with temperatures exceeding 95 degrees, making daytime collecting uncomfortable in the exposed badlands terrain. Winter is possible but early mornings can be cold.

faq.question: How far is Courthouse Wash from Moab and what kind of vehicle do you need?
faq.answer: Courthouse Wash is approximately 17 miles north of Moab via US 191 and State Road 313. A standard passenger vehicle with moderate ground clearance can reach the pullout in most conditions. The road is paved to the access area, though the final approach dirt road may have light washboard. 4x4 vehicles provide better access to side drainages if conditions are muddy.

faq.question: Is Courthouse Wash good for beginners and kids rockhounding in Utah?
faq.answer: Yes, Courthouse Wash is excellent for beginners and families. The terrain is easy to moderate with a flat wash floor, no technical climbing required, and abundant loose specimens accessible without specialized tools. Children ages 6 and up can successfully hunt for agates and jasper pieces. The scenic badlands setting and accessible location make it popular for family outings.

faq.question: What tools and gear should you bring rockhounding at Courthouse Wash?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots for uneven sandy and rocky terrain, sun protection including hat and sunscreen, and at least 2-3 liters of water per person. A small pry bar or geologist's pick helps loosen nodules from soft sediment, though most material can be collected by hand. A collection bag or backpack is essential to stay within the 25 lb daily limit. Binoculars can help spot specimen-bearing layers on the distant wash walls.

meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and chalcedony at Courthouse Wash near Moab. BLM public land with no permit required. Easy access, family-friendly collecting.
